figures.caption: FIG. 1. Normalized triangular geometries used in this work; hp/hs = 1.5 is shown for illustration (hp is the triangle height). The axes are ξ = (x− xapex)/hs and η = (y − yb)/hs, so the numerical coordinate scaling used in the DSMC input files does not affect the geometry supplied to the surrogates. The base length is unity in these coordinates.
TABLE I. Supported sampling topology of the DSMC database. The validation and interpretation in this paper are restricted to these sampled directions.

FIG. 2. Separation of the two computational roles in this study. Panel (a) summarizes the geometry-consistent coordinate-conditioned surrogates used to reconstruct bulk fields and direct wall-load profiles. Panel (b) shows the primary observability diagnostic, which is trained and evaluated on raw DSMC bulk neighborhoods and DSMC wall loads. The colored annuli in panel (b) denote nested neighborhoods sampled from the raw DSMC fields, and the magenta marker denotes the wall evaluation point s. Surrogate-reconstructed neighborhoods are introduced only in a separate closed-loop preservation test; they do not define the reference R95 values.
TABLE II. Mean validation relative L2 errors in percent for supported hold-out groups.

FIG. 3. Temperature validation for the Mach–rarefaction–orientation holdout: BWD protrusion, Mach number 6, Knudsen number 0.33, height ratio 1.5, and wall-temperature ratio 1. Temperature color scales are in kelvin, and the error panel is in percent of the robust DSMC range. The model reproduces the hot region generated by compression upstream of the protrusion; the largest error remains localized near the apex and near-wall recovery region.

FIG. 4. Temperature validation for the height-interpolation holdout: FWD protrusion with hp/hs = 1.25 at Mach number 6 and Knudsen number 0.33. Temperature color scales are in kelvin. The FWD windward face generates a compact thermal high upstream of the apex, and the corrected geometry prevents the field from entering the triangular solid.

FIG. 5. Temperature validation for the wall-temperature interpolation holdout: ISO protrusion with Tw/T∞ = 2 at Mach number 6 and Knudsen number 0.1. Temperature color scales are in kelvin. The surrogate captures both the upstream high-temperature band and its downstream extension, showing that the thermal sensitivity is learned in the sampled wall-temperature direction.

FIG. 6. Streamwise-velocity validation for the three representative holdout cases. Row captions below each panel identify the physical validation condition. Velocity color scales are in m s−1. The model captures the broad acceleration/deceleration structure, while errors remain concentrated near the wall and apex.

FIG. 7. Transverse-velocity validation for the same holdout cases. Velocity color scales are in m s−1. This component is physically tied to streamline deflection and weak recirculation; therefore, it is more sensitive to small positional errors in the shear layer and recovery region.

FIG. 8. Pressure validation using the pressure-focused surrogate. Pressure color scales are in pascals. The pressure model is trained on logarithmic pressure with emphasis on high-pressure, near-wall, and apex-adjacent cells. This specialization improves the peak-compression region relative to a single multi-output field network.

FIG. 9. Surface-only validation of protrusion-wall Cp, Cq, and |τ |. DSMC curves are shown without post-hoc smoothing so that particle-sampling fluctuations and localized extrema remain visible. The wall-surface surrogate is trained on face-aware wall features rather than on the bulk field. The agreement is strongest on the windward rise and peak region, which are the most important parts of the protrusion-wall response for pressure and thermal loading. In this figure, “NN” denotes the neural-network wall-surface surrogate.
TABLE IV. Bulk-to-wall prediction errors and reductions. Values are mean LOOCV relative L2 errors in percent across the 27 independent physical conditions.

FIG. 10. Mean leave-one-physical-case-out (LOOCV) relative wall-profile error versus model input and observed bulk-field radius. The vertically stacked panels show Cp, Cq, and |τ |. The horizontal axis ranges from parameter-only and wall-local baselines to cumulative radii R/hs to the fulldomain descriptor; the vertical axis shows the mean relative L2 error. The gap between parameteronly and bulk-aware models demonstrates that operating parameters alone do not determine the wall profiles.

TABLE V. Compact robustness controls for the predictive-support diagnostic. Error reductions compare parameter-only and full-domain inputs within independently refitted models. Supportlength ranges use the full-domain ring reference. For these controls, Rϵ denotes the smallest tested radius satisfying E(R) ≤ (1 + ϵ)E(ref), so that R95 corresponds to the special case ϵ = 0.05.

FIG. 12. Closed-loop preservation of radius-dependent wall-load error. The vertically stacked panels show Cp, Cq, and |τ |; the horizontal axis follows the same input/radius sequence as Fig. 10, and the vertical axis is median relative error. Diagnostic regressors are trained only on raw DSMC descriptors from non-held-out cases. Blue curves evaluate independent holdouts with raw DSMC descriptors, whereas red curves replace only the held-out bulk field by its coordinate-conditioned surrogate reconstruction. Shaded bands show the 2.5–97.5 percentile range over 20 diagnostic random seeds.

TABLE VI. Closed-loop preservation of the model-conditional information footprint. ∆R95 is surrogate minus raw DSMC in units of hs; ∆Efull is the increase in full-domain relative error in percentage points. Brackets give 2.5–97.5 percentile intervals over 20 diagnostic random seeds.

FIG. 13. Knudsen-number dependence of the lower-bound information horizon. Filled symbols denote finite R95 values and open overlays identify Knudsen-number/orientation groups containing right-censored cases. The curves are lower-bound averages because censored cases are assigned R95/hs = 3 for plotting. The observed variation is quantity- and orientation-dependent; the figure is interpreted as lower-bound predictive support rather than as a universal Knudsen-number scaling.

TABLE VII. Casewise information-horizon statistics by wall quantity and orientation. Censored cases satisfy R95/hs > 3; the lower-bound mean assigns them the value 3 and is therefore conservative. The reported horizons are single-fit point estimates; the diagnostic-regressor uncertainty for Cq is wide and reaches the censoring limit for the backward-facing orientation (Fig. 14), so the trailing digit should not be read as statistically resolved.

FIG. 14. Tree-bootstrap sensitivity of the lower-bound heat-transfer information horizon. Points show the median orientation-specific mean R95/hs and bars show 2.5–97.5 percentile intervals from 50 bootstrap resamples of the diagnostic trees. The reduced uncertainty experiment uses R/hs = 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 3 and quantifies diagnostic-regressor uncertainty only, not independent DSMC particle-sampling uncertainty.
TABLE X. Entropy-index and peak-location validation. S denotes ∆seq/Rg; range mean absolute error is normalized by the robust DSMC entropy range. Pressure is normalized.

FIG. 15. Local-equilibrium entropy-rise proxy for the Mach–rarefaction–orientation validation cases. The solid triangle is masked, and the error is reported as a percent of the robust DSMC entropy-index range. The main entropy-rise structure is reproduced, while the residual error is concentrated around the apex. Here “ML” denotes the entropy proxy reconstructed from the machine-learning surrogates for T and P .

FIG. 16. Local-equilibrium entropy-rise proxy for height-interpolation validation cases. The comparison shows that the separately trained temperature and pressure surrogates remain mutually consistent when combined in a thermodynamic post-processing diagnostic.
TABLE XI. Selected region-conditioned relative L2 errors in percent.
TABLE XII. Coefficient of determination between DSMC-derived and predicted engineering QoIs. Values close to one indicate strong parity.
TABLE XIII. Ablation and design-decision summary.

TABLE XIV. Summary of the supplied DSMC execution records. Particle populations and reported runtimes are shown as minimum–median–maximum. The full case-level record is distributed with the reproducibility package.
